facts: A single cubic centimeter (cm³) of Earth's atmosphere holds ~2.5 x 10¹⁹ molecules.
The best vacuum labs on Earth can achieve contains ~10,000 molecules per cubic centimeter.
In the 'empty' void between stars, the average density drops to just ~1 hydrogen atom per cubic centimeter.
But in the vast intergalactic voids, it's a staggering ~1 atom per *cubic meter*.
idea.payoff: The average intergalactic void is 1,000,000 times emptier than interstellar space and far beyond any vacuum we can create!
idea.title: Space isn't just empty, it's INCREDIBLY empty! 🤯

scenes.visual: Vast, dark cosmos with faint, distant stars.

scenes.text:
Earth's atmosphere: ~2.5x10¹⁹ molecules/cm³
Best lab vacuum: ~10,000 molecules/cm³
scenes.visual: Starts with Earth's atmosphere, then zooms into a lab setting with a tiny glowing cubic centimeter.
A serene, distant nebula background with a single, barely visible dot representing an atom in a cubic centimeter outline.

scenes.text: INTERGALACTIC voids: ~1 atom per CUBIC METER
scenes.visual: Expansive, dark void with extremely distant, tiny galaxies. A large, translucent cubic meter outline appears with a single, tiny dot inside.

scenes.text:
Intergalactic voids are 1,000,000x emptier than interstellar space!
Far beyond any vacuum we can create!
scenes.visual: A visually striking comparison of two cubes: one tiny and dense, the other vast and almost empty, set against the deepest, darkest cosmic background.

youtube.title: Space isn't just empty, it's INCREDIBLY empty! 🤯
